Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Deposition Corrections
Off Calendar Jul-23-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom Conference
Stephen J. Wiard, Esq. FEHER LAW, APC (310) 340-1112 stephen@feherlawfirm.com Attorney for Plaintiff, DAVE JOLLY
JOSHUA D. KIENITZ, Esq., LITTLER MENDELSON, P.C. 925.932.2468 jkienitz@littler.com Attorney for Defendant PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY

Counsel for defendant PG&E seeks clarification of the Court's tenative ruling re: summary judgment.

Hearing is held. discussed, not argued. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC'S Motion To Strike Deposition Corrections

Defendant PG&E's motion to strike plaintiff Dave Jolly's deposition corrections is denied without prejudice as unripe.

PG&E contends that a series of corrections Jolly made to his deposition transcript are actually substantive contradictions that should be stricken. PG&E relies on cases where trial courts have disregarded purported corrections in deciding summary judgment motions. (See Gray v. Reeves (1977) 76 Cal.App.3d 567, 574; Leasman v. Beech Aircraft Corp. (1975) 48 Cal.App.3d 376, 382 [citing summary judgment rule that "when a defendant can establish his defense with the plaintiff's admissions sufficient to pass the strict construction test imposed on the moving party (citations), the credibility of the admissions are valued so highly that the controverting affidavits may be disregarded as irrelevant, inadmissible or evasive"]; Tiffany Builders, LLC v. Delrahim (2023) 97 Cal.App.5th 536, 548; Vulk v. State Farm General Insurance Company (2021) 69 Cal.App.5th 243, 258.) The language of "striking" a correction is used in some of these cases, but what they really mean is that the correction is disregarded for purposes of determining whether there are triable issues of fact. PG&E points to no authority that this court has the freestanding power to strike portions of a deposition such that they cannot be used by anyone for any purpose. (The federal case PG&E relies on, Hambleton Bros. Lumber Co. v. Balkin Enterprises, Inc. (9th Cir. 2005) 397 F.3d 1217, 1227, is slightly different; it appears that federal courts recognize a freestanding motion to strike any untimely or improper deposition corrections under Fed. R. Civ. P. 30, a rule that does not apply to this proceeding.)

Moreover, if the case proceeds to trial and deposition testimony is used at trial, there is authority that both the corrected and uncorrected versions can go before the jury and the jury can decide what to make of the fact that a deponent offered a correction. (See George v. Double-D Foods, Inc. (1984) 155 Cal.App.3d 36, 45.) In other words, even contradictory testimony is not "stricken" in the sense that it cannot be used; instead, another party can introduce evidence of the contradiction, and the factfinder may draw an adverse inference from the fact of the change.

The court thus concludes that it does not have freestanding authority to strike contradictory deposition corrections under the sham affidavit doctrine. The difference between the deposition testimony and the corrections will become material only at summary judgment and at trial. Because this court does not have a summary judgment motion before it now, it has no occasion or basis to evaluate whether the corrections are contradictory or not. Similarly, the question of what deposition testimony can be heard by the jury (if any) is a question reserved for the trial court.

PG&E asks in the alternative for leave to file supplemental briefing on its summary judgment motion to address the corrections. The court denies this request; there is no pending summary judgment motion on this court's calendar and in any event the procedural statute concerning how to make and oppose summary judgment motions is strictly construed and does not provide
for supplemental

Order is signed.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)

Off Calendar Apr-02-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Sandy T. Luu, Esq. FEHER LAW, APC (310) 340-1112 sandy@feherlawfirm.com Attorneys for Plaintiff, DAVE JOLLY (via ZOOM)
WILLIAM A. COSMOPULOS, Esq., LITTLER MENDELSON, P.C. 415.433.1940 bcosmopulos@littler.com
Attorney for Defendants PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY and ERIC WACKER

Hearing is held. Argued. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC'S Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Further Deposition Testimony

Defendant Pacific Gas and Electric Company's Renewed Motion to Compel Plaintiff's Further Deposition Testimony and Sanctions is granted in part and denied in part.

PG&E took five hours of plaintiff Dave Jolly's deposition testimony. When the deposition did not finish at the end of one business day, and the captioner whose participation was necessary was not available the next business day, PG&E stated its intent to keep the deposition open and Jolly's counsel stated his position that the deposition could continue the next day (under the terms of the notice that the deposition would occur "from day to day until complete") but that Jolly would not appear on a future date, even if the deposition were remote. (Cosmopulos Dec., Ex. E, p. 169-170.) PG&E noticed a continued deposition, and Jolly objected and refused to appear on a subsequent day. (Cosmopulos Dec. Exs. F, I.)

PG&E now moves to compel what it deems the completion of the deposition and what Jolly deems another deposition in contravention of the one-deposition rule (CCP 2025.610(a).) The court grants the motion. The court agrees with Jolly that the deposition is untimely if brought as a motion to compel compliance with the first deposition notice pursuant to 2025.480(b); such a motion was due 60 days after the record was completed, on or about April 28, 2024. But PG&E served a second notice of deposition for a second day of testimony, timely filing its motion based on that notice, and alternatively argues there is good cause for more than one deposition of Jolly under 2025.610(b). The court agrees. The court cannot say under the circumstances here that it is so clear that PG&E should have been prepared with a captioner on the second day that its failure to do so amounts to forfeiture of its opportunity to complete its deposition of the plaintiff in this case. In any event, the limited time PG&E had with Plaintiff in the first session and the additional materials that have been subsequently produced warrant further testimony. The court orders Plaintiff to appear for a second day of deposition, on a date mutually agreed by the parties within 30 days of entry of this order. The deposition shall be remote unless PG&E pays travel costs for Jolly and his counsel. PG&E shall provide a captioner or similar accommodation.

Both parties seek sanctions pursuant to 2025.480(j). Although Jolly opposed the motion, his position was warranted by a reasonable reading of 2025.480. The court finds substantial justification and does not award sanctions.

Order is signed.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)
